summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should use Exception.ToString() instead of Exception.Message when handling errors, as the former provides crucial debugging information like stack traces. He extends this principle to a broader design guideline: every object should implement a meaningful ToString() method that provides useful information rather than just echoing the class name. Using DataSet as a prime example of poor ToString() implementation, he demonstrates how the default behavior returns useless output when it could display the actual data in a readable format.
key_points: Exception.Message loses valuable debugging context like stack traces; Exception.ToString() should be the default choice
Every custom object should implement a meaningful ToString() that represents what the object actually contains
Microsoft's own DataSet class violates this principle by returning just 'System.Data.DataSet' instead of its contents
Good ToString() implementations require extra work but provide debugging value that should have been built-in
money_quotes: Unless there's some compelling reason you need an ultra-terse version of the error, it's almost always better to use the provided Exception.ToString() method.
Any object you build should have a meaningful .ToString() method.
I expect a proper string representation of what you are, not a meaningless echo of your class name!
Seems perfectly logical to me, but you'll have to write your own custom ToString() implementation to get the behavior that should have been there in the first place.
key_insight: A well-implemented ToString() method that reveals an object's actual state is essential for debugging and should be a standard practice, not an afterthought.
